title: The role of the periaqueductal gray in freeze and collapse responses
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The tiny structure at the center of your survival

There is a part of your brain roughly the size of a pea. It sits deep in your brainstem, wrapped around the cerebral aqueduct like a sleeve. It is called the periaqueductal gray, or PAG. And it is the difference between freezing in a meeting and calmly excusing yourself. It is the difference between collapsing in exhaustion and taking a nap. It is the difference between feeling safe in your body and feeling like you are trapped inside a cage made of your own muscles.

I know this sounds dramatic. But I have spent years studying this structure, reading the work of researchers like Stephen Porges and Peter Levine, and I can tell you without hesitation: the PAG is where your survival instincts live. It is the switchboard that decides whether you fight, flee, freeze, or collapse. And most of us never even know it exists.

Here's the thing. The PAG doesn't care about your goals for the day. It doesn't care about your to-do list or your relationship or your career. It cares about one thing only: keeping you alive. And it makes that decision in milliseconds, based on sensory input you are not even conscious of. It reads your body's signals, your environment's cues, and your nervous system's history, and then it selects a survival response. Just like that. No deliberation. No consultation with your prefrontal cortex. No asking for your opinion.

Wild, right?

Freeze: the pause before the storm

Let's talk about freeze first. Because freeze is not collapse, and confusing the two is dangerous.

Freeze is a state of high arousal held in place. Your muscles are tense. Your heart rate may be elevated. Your breath is shallow and quick. You are alert, hypervigilant, scanning for threat. But you are not moving. You are still. Paralyzed. Like a deer in headlights, but with all the engine revving.

This is the PAG at work. Specifically, the ventrolateral PAG (vlPAG) and the lateral PAG (lPAG) are both active during freeze. The lPAG is associated with active defense, the kind that wants to fight or flee but can't. The vlPAG is more about passive coping, the kind that says "if I don't move, maybe it won't see me." Together, they create a state of immobilized vigilance. You are stuck. But you are still online. Still trying. Still scanning for escape.

I have been there. Many times. In a conversation that turned into a confrontation, my body would lock. My voice would disappear. My limbs would feel like lead. But inside, my heart was pounding. My mind was racing. I was not calm. I was not relaxed. I was frozen in a state of extreme alertness, unable to act, but fully aware of the danger.

Peter Levine calls this the "freeze response" and he has written extensively about how it gets stuck in the body. In his work, he describes how the energy of freeze must be released through gentle, titrated movement and sensation. If you do not release it, it stays. It becomes a pattern. It becomes the way you respond to stress, even when the stress is mild.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link). That book explores how these survival patterns can be passed down through generations, wired into your nervous system before you even take your first breath.

Collapse: when the system gives up

Collapse is different. And I need you to understand this difference, because the wellness industry loves to conflate them. They will tell you to "rest" when you are in collapse, but collapse is not rest. It is not restorative. It is not healing.

Collapse is a state of low arousal. Your heart rate drops. Your blood pressure drops. Your muscles go limp. You feel heavy, numb, disconnected. You might feel like you are floating outside your body, or like the world is far away. You are not alert. You are not scanning. You have given up. The PAG has made a decision: there is no way out. Active defense is useless. Passive coping is useless. The only option left is to shut down.

This is the dorsal PAG (dPAG) and the deep layers of the superior colliculus getting involved. The dPAG is associated with quiescence, immobility, and pain modulation. When the dPAG takes over, it is like the system has pulled the emergency brake. Everything slows. Everything numbs. You dissociate. You collapse.

I have been here too. After a particularly bad period of chronic stress, I remember lying on my couch for hours, unable to move. Not because I was tired. Not because I was relaxing. Because my body had decided that the safest thing to do was to become a stone. To feel nothing. To need nothing. To be nothing.

That is not rest. That is survival. And it comes at a cost.

Stephen Porges, in his polyvagal theory, describes this as the dorsal vagal state. It is the oldest, most primitive part of our nervous system. It is the response of the lizard brain, the one that plays dead when the predator is too close. And when it becomes chronic, it leads to depression, chronic fatigue, autoimmune issues, and a sense of disconnection from life.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Gabor Mate writes about how the suppression of stress responses, including collapse, can as physical disease.

The PAG as a survival switchboard

So how does the PAG decide between freeze and collapse? It is not random. It is based on a calculation that happens below your awareness.

The PAG receives input from the amygdala, the insula, the prefrontal cortex, and the hypothalamus. It reads your internal state: heart rate, blood pressure, respiration, pain signals. It reads your external environment: sounds, sights, smells, the presence or absence of safety cues. And then it runs a cost-benefit analysis. Can you fight? Can you flee? If yes, the lPAG and dPAG activate for active defense. If no, but there is still hope, the vlPAG activates for freeze. If there is no hope at all, the dPAG takes over for collapse.

This is not a conscious process. You cannot think your way out of it. You cannot "choose" to fight if your PAG has decided you cannot win. You cannot "choose" to rest if your PAG has decided you need to collapse. Your survival system is older and faster than your thinking brain. It will override your intentions every time.

And here is the uncomfortable truth: if you have a history of trauma, your PAG's calculation is biased. It has learned that threat is everywhere. It has learned that you are weak, that you cannot win, that the best strategy is to freeze or collapse. It has made these decisions so many times that they have become automatic. Your PAG is not responding to the present moment. It is responding to a past that is still alive in your nervous system.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). Peter Levine walks through how the body holds these patterns and how we can gently release them.

What recovery looks like for the PAG

Recovery is not about bypassing the PAG. It is not about "thinking positive" or "ing safety." It is about retraining the PAG's calculation. And that takes time. And patience. And a lot of body-based work.

Here is what I have learned from my own recovery and from the research of Janina Fisher and Pat Ogden.

First, you have to learn to track your state. Not judge it. Not fix it. Just notice. When do you freeze? When do you collapse? What are the internal signals? A tight chest. A hollow stomach. A sense of pressure behind your eyes. Numbness in your hands. These are the PAG's messages. Listen to them without trying to change them.

Second, you have to build capacity for activation without collapse. This means intentionally moving in and out of states. Gently. Slowly. You might stand up and feel your feet. You might take a deep breath and notice the sensation. You might rock side to side. These small movements send a message to the PAG: you are not trapped. You can move. You have options.

Third, you have to create safety in your environment. Not just physical safety, but relational safety. The PAG is highly sensitive to social cues. A kind voice. A gentle touch. A familiar face. These signals can shift the PAG's calculation from threat to safety. This is why connection is so important. This is why isolation is so damaging.

Fourth, you have to accept that some responses will remain. You cannot erase your PAG's history. You can only add new data. You can only build new patterns. And even then, under extreme stress, the old responses may come back. That is not failure. That is biology.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). Deb Dana offers tools for building ventral vagal safety, which can help shift the PAG's default settings over time.

The open ending

I do not have a neat conclusion for you. I cannot tell you that you will never freeze or collapse again. That would be a lie. The PAG is not going anywhere. It is part of your survival system, and it will always be there, ready to protect you.

But here is what I can tell you. The more you understand your PAG, the less afraid you will be of its responses. The less you will shame yourself for freezing in a conversation. The less you will judge yourself for collapsing after a stressful week. You will recognize these states for what they are: ancient, intelligent, survival strategies. Not weaknesses. Not failures. Not signs that you are broken.

And from that place of understanding, you can start to work with your nervous system instead of against it. You can learn to breathe into the freeze. You can learn to gently move out of the collapse. You can build a relationship with the pea-sized structure in your brainstem that has been keeping you alive all along.

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • It Didn't Start with You (paid link) - Explores inherited trauma patterns and how they shape your nervous system.
  • When the Body Says No (paid link) - Connects the dots between suppressed stress responses and physical illness.
  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - A body-based approach to releasing trauma from the nervous system.
  • Anchored (paid link) - Practical tools for building safety and shifting your nervous system's default responses.
